data.title: SSR Mining to sell Copler mine to Turkish firm in $1.5B deal
data.description:
SSR Mining (SSRM) +6.4% pre-market Wednesday after saying it agreed to sell its 80% ownership stake in the Copler mine and related properties in Turkey to Cengiz Holding for $1.5B in cash.

The Copler mine and related properties encompass all mining licenses, assets, rights, liabilities, and interests relating to or in connection with the company's operations in eastern Anatolia.

SSR Mining (SSRM) said it is conducting a strategic review of its remaining platform in Turkey, including the 20% earned interest in the Hod Maden development project.

"With last year's acquisition of the Cripple Creek & Victor mine and today's agreement for the sale of Copler, we have strategically repositioned the portfolio to the Americas. As the third largest gold producer in the United States, SSR Mining is expected to continue its robust annual production, anchored by operations in the USA," Executive Chairman Rod Antal said.

data.title: GM and Ford are top picks at BofA in the 'Year of the Pickup'
data.description:
Bank of America thinks the auto industry will outperform expectations this year as automakers adjust to a new regulatory environment that favors their higher-margin accretive internal combustion engine vehicles. The firm highlighted  Ford Motor (F) and General Motors (GM) as top sector picks. 

Analyst Alexander Perry and his team see the potential for upward estimate revisions given the shift away from electric vehicle and emissions mandates that limited profitability over the past several years.

Notably, 2026 was called the year of the pickup, as U.S. OEMs are seen being in a more favorable position to prioritize the production of their most margin-accretive gas-powered vehicles, especially pickup trucks, while delaying or cancelling lower-margin electric vehicle models. "We expect EV sales to decline 20%+ in 2026 from the phaseout of consumer incentives, while automakers’ cancellation of 40% of EV programs and extension of over 45% of ICE programs will pressure penetration over the next several years," wrote Perry.

General Motors (GM) was assigned a Buy rating by BofA as a key beneficiary of recent regulatory changes, including the removal of CAFÉ penalties and greenhouse gas relief, which is enabling GM (GM) to mix shift to its most margin-accretive trucks/SUVs and away from unprofitable electric vehicles in the near term.

Ford Motor (F) also earned a Buy rating as a more favorable regulatory backdrop in the U.S. is expected to enable the Detroit automaker to mix shift to its most margin-accretive truck/SUV platforms.

Shares of General Motors (GM) were up 0.8% in premarket trading and are more than 60% higher over the last 52 weeks. Ford Motor (F) rose 0.9% in the early session and is 35% higher over the last year.

data.title: Box outlines 8% revenue growth target for FY 2027 with Enterprise Advanced driving 10% of revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Box, Inc. (BOX) Q4 2026

Management View

  • CEO Aaron Levie reported, "We delivered strong Q4 operating results, reflecting continued growth in customer demand for Box AI and the success of our Enterprise Advanced offering. We achieved revenue of $306 million, up 9% year-over-year or 8% in constant currency and Q4 EPS of $0.49, above our guidance." Levie highlighted the launch and growth of Enterprise Advanced, which now accounts for 10% of company revenue, and emphasized its traction with customer wins across key industries including biotech, robotics, insurance, and government regulatory bodies.
  • Levie discussed the critical role of enterprise content in AI transformation, stating, "Nearly every enterprise leader that I talk to today is looking to transform how their company operates with AI… Much of that unique context lives inside of enterprise content." He announced new products such as Box Extract, enabling AI-powered data extraction, and Box Shield Pro for advanced security. Strategic partnerships were highlighted, including launches with Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.5 and 4.6, Google Gemini 3.0 Flash, and OpenAI GPT-5.2 in Box AI Studio.
  • For FY '27, Levie outlined plans to deliver "the next generation of AI agent features within Box," including Box Automate, which will combine human and agent-powered workflows and expand Box Extract's capabilities. He also pointed to a major focus on API monetization as enterprises centralize content for AI use cases.
  • CFO Dylan Smith stated, "In FY '26, we delivered revenue of $1.18 billion, up 8% year-over-year and up 7% in constant currency. We drove an acceleration in RPO growth to 17% year-over-year or 16% in constant currency. Operating margin came in at 28.3%, up 50 basis points year-over-year and up 40 basis points in constant currency. Finally, in FY '26, we generated record free cash flow of $313 million, up 3% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Smith provided guidance for Q1 FY '27, expecting revenue of $304 million, representing approximately 10% year-over-year growth or 9% in constant currency. Q1 EPS is expected at $0.36. For the full fiscal year ending January 31, 2027, Smith stated, "we expect our full year revenue to be approximately $1.275 billion, representing 8% year-over-year growth or 9% in constant currency." Full-year EPS is expected to be approximately $1.55, or $1.58 in constant currency. Box anticipates continued investment in go-to-market initiatives and product development, with "operating margin to be approximately 28% or 28.5% in constant currency." Management also flagged a $3 million expense shift into Q4 due to the timing of BoxWorks.

Financial Results

  • Box reported Q4 revenue of $306 million and EPS of $0.49. Customers paying at least $100,000 annually grew 9% year-over-year. Remaining performance obligations (RPO) reached $1.7 billion, a 17% year-over-year increase. Total Suites customers now represent 66% of revenue, up from 60% a year ago. Gross margin for Q4 was 82.3%. Box generated Q4 free cash flow of $98 million and ended with $480 million in cash and investments. Share repurchases totaled 4.4 million shares for $126 million in Q4 and 9.7 million shares for $293 million in FY '26.

Q&A

  • Steven Enders, Citi: Asked about the opportunity and threat from AI, and how changes in the GenAI landscape impact Box's content layer. Levie responded that "more software is just good for us because all of that software needs to eventually probably touch some type of unstructured data in an enterprise context" and described Box as well-positioned due to the need for secure content management for both people and AI agents.
  • Enders followed up on Enterprise Advanced expectations for FY '27. Smith noted ongoing momentum and a 30% to 40% pricing uplift for Enterprise Advanced over Enterprise Plus, adding, "we've been really pleased with just how much the value of these newer capabilities are resonating with customers."
  • Rishi Jaluria, RBC: Asked about AI adoption in regulated industries. Levie said, "we are 100% focused on applied AI use cases in an organization," emphasizing data extraction and workflow automation as key drivers of adoption.
  • Brian Peterson, Raymond James: Inquired about demand linearity and geographic performance. Smith reported strong performance in both commercial and enterprise segments, with particular strength in Japan and U.S. regions. Peterson also asked about Enterprise Advanced ramp. Levie said it supports both upsells and new customer conversations, especially in workflow automation.
  • Matthew Bullock, BofA: Asked about net revenue retention components and API monetization. Smith explained improvements from both pricing and net seat expansion and said platform monetization will contribute to NRR over time.
  • Lucky Schreiner, D.A. Davidson: Asked about Enterprise Advanced early vs. late adopters. Levie noted improvements in AI model accuracy have expanded the range of workflows that can be automated, driving broader adoption.
  • Jason Ader, William Blair: Sought response to SaaS bear narratives. Levie stated, "there's almost nothing in that, that is bad for Box," emphasizing the need for shared file systems for both agents and people regardless of seat-based model evolution. Ader also asked about API monetization, to which Levie outlined dual monetization levers: seat and consumption-based pricing.
  • Seth Gilbert, UBS: Explored $100,000+ customer growth drivers. Levie attributed growth to Enterprise Advanced and the platform's AI capabilities. Gilbert also asked about conditions for future margin expansion. Smith said, "there's nothing -- no required growth rate to be improving operating margin at a greater clip… most of those investments on the sales and marketing side."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was positive, with questions focused on product momentum, AI adoption, and monetization strategies. No signs of skepticism or pressing negativity emerged, and analysts congratulated management multiple times.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone throughout, both in prepared remarks and responses, repeatedly using phrases like "we are incredibly excited" and "we are confident."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment showed a stronger sense of momentum, especially around Enterprise Advanced and AI-driven growth levers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • FY '27 guidance shows an increase in revenue and EPS targets compared to prior guidance for FY '26. Management moved from discussing product launches to highlighting early traction and monetization with Enterprise Advanced, now at 10% of revenue versus continued adoption in the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward AI agent enablement, workflow automation, and content platform centralization. Analysts' inquiries in the current quarter were more focused on monetization and adoption of new AI-driven features, compared to broader questions about market opportunity and go-to-market efficiency in Q3.
  • Management's tone in Q4 was more assertive on Box's competitive positioning in the AI era versus the more measured optimism of Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management called out FX headwinds in Q1 FY '27, particularly a 530 basis point headwind from U.S. dollar to yen movement, and a 100 basis point headwind for the full year.
  • Ongoing investments in go-to-market and product initiatives could constrain operating margin in the near term as Box aims to capture market opportunity.
  • No material concerns were raised by analysts regarding product adoption or competitive pressures, and management did not note any significant operational risks beyond currency impacts and expense seasonality from BoxWorks.

Final Takeaway

Box closed FY '26 with strong revenue growth, record free cash flow, and significant adoption of Enterprise Advanced, which now represents 10% of revenue. For FY '27, management expects continued growth, targeting $1.275 billion in revenue and $1.55 EPS, while investing in AI agent features, workflow automation, and platform monetization. The company’s leadership highlighted increasing customer demand for AI-powered solutions and emphasized Box’s unique positioning as the content platform for both human and AI agent workflows, while maintaining disciplined cost controls and a robust capital allocation strategy.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Latham outlines 2026 guidance targeting $580M–$610M net sales amid Sand States push and Freedom Pools acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Latham Group, Inc. (SWIM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Sean Gadd, President and CEO, opened his first earnings call as CEO highlighting "strong results that the company achieved in both the fourth quarter and the full year of 2025" and emphasized that "fourth quarter revenues were up 15%, showing a solid growth across all of our product lines."
  • Gadd reported that In-Ground Pool sales for the year were "1% above 2024 levels" despite industry-wide declines, attributing this to "increased demand for Latham's fiberglass pools," which represented 76.5% of In-Ground Pool sales in 2025. He noted, "fiberglass pool sales of approximately 2.5%" year-on-year growth and observed that "fiberglass... gained another percentage point of market share in 2025 to account for approximately 24% of last year's U.S. pool starts."
  • Covers and liners products contributed positively, with "22% growth in autocover sales in 2025" and liner sales up 4%, aided by the rollout of an AI-powered measuring tool. Gadd highlighted the strategic expansion into the Sand States, stating the company "gained considerable ground in FRGP, our initial target market, achieving double-digit sales growth for the year."
  • Gadd revealed the completion of an acquisition: "a few days ago, we completed an acquisition that meets all 3 criteria. Oliver will provide more details shortly, from my perspective, Freedom Pools represent excellent acquisition," which expands Latham's market in Australia and New Zealand and is "immediately accretive to our earnings."
  • CFO Oliver Gloe stated, "our full year 2025 sales exceeded the midpoint of our guidance range, while our adjusted EBITDA performance was above our guidance range, demonstrating the benefits of volume leverage and production efficiencies." Gloe reported, "Net sales for the fourth quarter of 2025 were $100 million, up 15% compared to EUR 87 million in the fourth quarter of 2024," and described margin expansion and cost discipline as key drivers.

Outlook

  • The company guided for 2026 net sales "between $580 million and $610 million" and adjusted EBITDA "between $105 million and $120 million," representing "year-on-year growth of 9% and 12.7%, respectively, at the midpoint." Gloe added this includes "mid-single-digit organic growth, together with the benefits from the Freedom Pools acquisition and consider increased marketing expenses."
  • Gadd described ongoing tough market conditions but stated, "we believe Laser is uniquely positioned to outperform the overall market once again," citing "category leadership in fiberglass pools and autocovers and the continued execution of our strategic priorities."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for the fourth quarter were reported at $100 million, with organic growth of 14% for the quarter. In-Ground Pool sales reached $50 million, cover sales $37 million, and liner sales $13 million for the quarter.
  • Gross margin expanded by 340 basis points to 28% in the fourth quarter, while SG&A expenses increased to $31 million, mainly from investments in sales, marketing, and personnel.
  • Net loss for the quarter was ($7 million) or ($0.06) per diluted share, compared to ($29 million) or ($0.25) per diluted share in Q4 2024. Adjusted EBITDA was $10 million for Q4 and $100 million for the full year.
  • Full year net sales were $546 million, up 7% from $509 million in the prior year. Gross margin for the year expanded 320 basis points to 33%. Year-end cash position was $71 million; net cash provided by operating activities was $51 million for the year; year-end total debt was $280 million with a net debt leverage ratio of 2.1.

Q&A

  • Greg Palm, Craig-Hallum Capital Group, asked about any change in strategy under new leadership. Gadd responded, "I'm very excited about what the opportunity looks like in the Sand States... that's really about lead generation, quality of leads, getting the brand where we wanted, at the same time, getting qualified dealers into the MPCs that are going to fulfill that demand."
  • Palm inquired about accelerating the conversion opportunity. Gadd emphasized "managing the installed cost of the job" and controlling the process end-to-end.
  • Timothy Wojs, Robert W. Baird & Co., asked about early demand indicators for 2026. Gadd replied, "tough market conditions, but feel good about what we can deliver in that environment."
  • Wojs also questioned margin expansion, to which Gloe said, "it will be a little bit more moderate, but our expectation is that we take a meaningful step towards that 35% gross margin."
  • Scott Stringer, Wolfe Research, discussed pricing power for 2026. Gloe stated, "price given us being the combination of both will probably be adding 2% to our top line."
  • Anika Dholakia, Barclays, asked about the long-term vision for the MPC strategy. Gadd explained the goal is to "earn your way to the space with national builders" and that the company is not yet ready to play at that level, but sees it as a future target.
  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs, explored dealer backlog and innovation momentum. Gadd said, "backlogs look pretty good. I think that the dealers are feeling relatively optimistic about where the year might be."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, probing on growth drivers, margin sustainability, and strategy under new leadership. Questions pressed for clarity on pricing power, dealer backlog, and expansion plans.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was optimistic and confident, as seen in statements like, "I see tremendous opportunity for Latham to grow in the years ahead." During Q&A, the tone remained constructive but became more measured, especially around market conditions and growth pacing, evident in phrases such as "tough market conditions, but feel good about what we can deliver in that environment."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence level was consistent, but the new CEO brought increased emphasis on targeted marketing and Sand States expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 was explicitly introduced, with a midpoint target of $595 million in net sales and $112.5 million in adjusted EBITDA, up from the prior quarter’s narrowed 2025 guidance of $540–$550 million in net sales and $92–$98 million in adjusted EBITDA.
  • Strategic focus intensified on Sand States expansion and acceleration of fiberglass pool adoption, building on momentum from Q3. The acquisition of Freedom Pools marks a notable shift, as does increased investment in marketing.
  • Analysts remained focused on growth drivers and market share gains, with recurring questions about capacity, dealer engagement, and margin expansion. Management’s tone stayed optimistic, with new leadership reinforcing prior strategies while signaling more targeted execution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged "tough market conditions" and flat industry pool starts as ongoing challenges.
  • Gadd highlighted consumer awareness and education as gaps, particularly the need to "ensure the homeowners gain awareness of the true benefits of fiberglass."
  • Gloe noted that the benefit from prior price increases and tariff mitigation would continue, but macroeconomic factors like interest rates and consumer confidence present uncertainty.

Final Takeaway

Latham’s fourth quarter and full year 2025 results showcased solid growth across product lines, significant margin expansion, and a successful push into key markets such as the Sand States and Australia/New Zealand through the Freedom Pools acquisition. With a new CEO, the company is set to leverage strategic investments in marketing and operational efficiency to target $580 million to $610 million in net sales and $105 million to $120 million in adjusted EBITDA for 2026, while continuing to drive fiberglass pool adoption and keep a sharp focus on dealer engagement and market development amid a challenging macro environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tesla gains after BofA jumps back in with a bull rating
data.description:
Bank of America picked up coverage on Tesla (TSLA) once again, reinstating the stock with a Buy rating. The firm called Tesla (TSLA) the current leader in consumer autonomy. 

"We expect TSLA to quickly become a leader in robotaxi services, given its ability to scale more profitably than competitors. We see autonomous vehicles spurring the next era of mobility and, as the most significant change agent in the Auto 2.0 landscape, offering consumers the prospect of saving time, safer travel, and more accessible transportation," updated analyst Alexander Perry.

Later this year, Tesla (TSLA) is expected to scale its robotaxis quickly, which now operate in San Francisco and Austin, with 7 additional markets in the first half of the year alone. Tesla's (TSLA) camera-only approach was noted to be technically harder but much cheaper and leveraging a consumer-fleet data engine. The strategy should allow the Austin-based company to scale more profitably compared to robotaxi competitors, while the lack of drivers gives it a cost advantage over rideshare players, according to Perry and his team. Meanwhile, the Optimus humanoid segment is valued at over $30B. 

BofA set a price objective of $460 based on its sum-of-the-parts analysis. 

Shares of Tesla (TSLA) were up 1.9% in premarket action to $399.96.

data.title: TELUS invests in AST SpaceMobile to launch space-based cellular service across Canada
data.description:
• TELUS (TU) and AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) have signed a commercial agreement to bring space-based cellular broadband to remote areas across Canada.
  • The service will combine AST SpaceMobile’s low-Earth-orbit satellite network with TELUS’ wireless network to provide coverage in remote locations across Canada.
  • As part of the deal, TELUS (TU) will invest in ground-based satellite infrastructure and take an equity stake in AST SpaceMobile (ASTS). Starting in late 2026, TELUS customers are expected to be able to send texts, make calls, and use data in areas where traditional cellular networks are unavailable.
  • ASTS shares rose +2.6% premarket.
